coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermally in- s itu grown MoS2 films over different conducting substrates as electrochemical-SERS biosensors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Rohit Kumar
Singh, Ankita
Mishra, Ashish Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Detection of organic pollutants and biomolecules at very low concentrations is a great challenge for protecting the environment and diagnosis of human health. Noble metal nanomaterials have been employed as active surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) substrates for such detection but suffer with high cost. The two-dimensional (2D) transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) can be used as cost-effective alternative SERS substrates for the detection of organic impurities and biomolecules. Here, we report the hydrothermally synthesized in-situ grown MoS2 films over conducting carbon paper (CCP) and fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass and used them as SERS substrates for the detection of Rhodamine 6G (R6G), vitamin B12 and bilirubin. We observe that hydrothermally in-situ grown MoS2 film over FTO coated glass (MoS2-FTO) shows a better SERS detection limit for studied analytes compared to the in-situ grown MoS2 film over conducting carbon paper (MoS2-CCP). We observe the detection of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin with maximum enhancement factors of 2.42 × 106, 4.18 × 102, and 2.78 × 106, respectively, using MoS2-FTO as SERS substrate. Furthermore, we demonstrate the electrochemical-SERS (EC-SERS) activity of prepared SERS substrates. The MoS2-FTO shows a better EC-SERS signal as compared to MoS2-CCP due to the high conductivity (maximum charge transfer at oxidation potential), strong chemical adsorption, change of orientation, and density of the molecules on the surface of the substrate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide
Rhodamine 6G
Vitamin B12
Bilirubin
Electrochemical-SERS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of MoS2 films over CCP and FTO coated glass substrates

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support of IIT (BHU). The work was financially supported by SERB, India (Grant no. CRG/2020/002186).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the synthesis of in-situ grown MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO via hydrothermal route.

SEM images of in-situ grown MoS2-CCP at (a) low and (b) high magnifications; SEM images of in-situ grown MoS2-FTO at (c) low and (d) high magnifications (e) Raman and (f) FTIR spectra of MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO.

SERS spectra of R6G molecules at different concentrations on MoS2 films over (a) CCP and (c) FTO coated glass; SERS peak intensity vs R6G concentrations for (b) MoS2-CCP and (d) MoS2-FTO using 532 nm excitation wavelength.

SERS spectra of vitamin B12 molecules on (a) MoS2-CCP and (c) MoS2-FTO substrate at different concentrations; SERS peak intensity vs vitamin B12 concentrations for (b) MoS2-CCP and (d) MoS2-FTO substrate using 532 nm excitation wavelength.

SERS spectra of bilirubin molecules on (a) MoS2-CCP and (c) MoS2-FTO at different concentrations; SERS peak intensity vs bilirubin concentrations for (b) MoS2-CCP and (d) MoS2-FTO using 633 nm excitation wavelength.

FTIR spectra of (a) bare R6G and R6G adsorbed on MoS2 surface; (b) bare vitamin B12 and vitamin B12 adsorbed on MoS2 surface; (c) bare bilirubin and bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2 surface, grown over CCP and FTO coated glass; (d) Schematic of CT mechanism for MoS2/analyte system.
(a) CV curves of MoS2-CCP in 0.1 M KCl with different analyte molecules. EC-SERS spectra of (b) R6G (c) vitamin B12, and (d) bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2-CCP.
(a) CV curves of MoS2-FTO in 0.1 M KCl with different analyte molecules. EC-SERS spectra of (b) R6G (c) vitamin B12, and (d) bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2-FTO.

Relation between applied electrochemical potential and Raman intensity for (a) MoS2-CCP, and (b) MoS2-FTO. (c) Schematic band diagram of the photon-driven charge transfer between MoS2 to analyte molecules in the EC-SERS system.
Comparison of the SERS detection limits of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin over different substrates.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermally in- s itu grown MoS2 films over different conducting substrates as electrochemical-SERS biosensors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University), Varanasi, 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology
Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University), Varanasi, 221005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of organic pollutants and biomolecules at very low concentrations is a great challenge for protecting the environment and diagnosis of human health. Noble metal nanomaterials have been employed as active surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) substrates for such detection but suffer with high cost. The two-dimensional (2D) transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) can be used as cost-effective alternative SERS substrates for the detection of organic impurities and biomolecules. Here, we report the hydrothermally synthesized in-situ grown MoS2 films over conducting carbon paper (CCP) and fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass and used them as SERS substrates for the detection of Rhodamine 6G (R6G), vitamin B12 and bilirubin. We observe that hydrothermally in-situ grown MoS2 film over FTO coated glass (MoS2-FTO) shows a better SERS detection limit for studied analytes compared to the in-situ grown MoS2 film over conducting carbon paper (MoS2-CCP). We observe the detection of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin with maximum enhancement factors of 2.42 × 106, 4.18 × 102, and 2.78 × 106, respectively, using MoS2-FTO as SERS substrate. Furthermore, we demonstrate the electrochemical-SERS (EC-SERS) activity of prepared SERS substrates. The MoS2-FTO shows a better EC-SERS signal as compared to MoS2-CCP due to the high conductivity (maximum charge transfer at oxidation potential), strong chemical adsorption, change of orientation, and density of the molecules on the surface of the substrate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectroscopy provides characteristic spectral information of the molecules, due to its capability of providing “fingerprinting” ability of molecule vibration and has a wide range of applications in biology, chemistry, and medicine [1,2]. However, at very low concentrations, it is difficult to detect the molecules because of the low Raman scattering cross-sectional area [3]. Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) is a simplified, flexible, non-destructive, and ultrasensitive analytical technique that can identify and efficiently detect organic dyes and biomolecules even up to a single molecule through their unique vibrational signatures and has been widely applied in various fields, such as chemical analysis, food security, and medical diagnostics [4,5]. The electromagnetic mechanism (EM) and the chemical mechanism (CM) have been primarily used to explain SERS enhancement. The laser-induced surface plasmon resonance of free charge carriers at the surface of the metal nanostructure enhances the SERS signal due to EM, while the charge transfer (CT) between molecules and SERS substrates is the foundation of the CM [6,7]. The most commonly developed SERS substrates are the metal nanostructures like Au, Ag, Cu, etc. owing to their strong plasmon resonance, while conventional semiconductors like ZnO, TiO2, CuO, etc. show lower enhancement factors due to the CM [8,9]. Unfortunately, due to the high cost of metal substrates and lower enhancement of conventional semiconductors, it is required to search new SERS candidate that features low cost, easy producibility with significantly high SERS signal enhancement capability [10,11]. The two-dimensional (2D) layered transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) like MoS2, WSe2, WTe2, NbS2, etc. have proven to be an effective SERS substrate, due to their affinity, large surface area, increased chemical reactivity and excellent conductivity as compared to bulk materials [12,13]. In these 2D materials, chemical enhancement occurs due to charge transfer between these nanomaterials and adsorbed analytes. Among TMDs, MoS2 features properties such as high carrier mobility, tunable bandgap, excellent biocompatibility, strong chemical stability, and fluorescence quenching capabilities, which can be utilized for SERS application. The enhanced SERS performance can be achieved for analyte sensing using MoS2 nanostructures as SERS substrate by improving its surface area, analyte molecule adsorption capacity, and light absorption capability. It's interesting to note that MoS2 has a weak van der Waals bonding between its layers and a strong covalent bonding between Mo and S atoms within each layer. The charge transfer mechanism of SERS activity is largely influenced by this weak van der Waals interaction and different functional groups [12]. Among various synthesis routes of MoS2 like mechanical exfoliation, chemical exfoliation, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), sputtering, etc., the hydrothermal process is known to be cost-effective and can generate different morphology and functional groups in MoS2 [14]. Appropriate morphology and suitable functional groups at the surface of MoS2 can provide a suitable platform for analyte adsorption and hence a better detection limit.

Among different organic pollutants, Rhodamine 6G (R6G) is a cationic dye which is highly water soluble, highly fluorescent and non-degradable, and commonly observed in discharged from chemical and biological industries. The long-term intake of R6G can cause various health problems such as lung cancer, skin contact, and high blood pressure [15]. Hence, its detection to the ultra-low concentration can be useful for a healthy human society. The detection of another toxic biomolecule i.e., free bilirubin is also necessary. A high level of free bilirubin (concentration >50 μM) can result in death, liver illness, and traumatic brain injuries, while on the other hand, low bilirubin levels are linked to diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The urgent diagnosis of jaundice requires quick monitoring of bilirubin. There have been few reports on the detection of R6G and bilirubin using 2D materials and their composites but improving the detection limit is still a task. Sun et al. have successfully demonstrated the SERS detection of R6G molecule of 10−6 M concentration using plasma modified MoS2 nanoflakes deposited on SiO2/Si substrate [16]. Quan et al. reported the detection limit of 10−8 M of bilirubin concentration in serum using nanocomposite of MoS2@ZnO@Ag [17]. Geng et al. prepared a modifying functional boron nitride (M − BN) monolayer nanosheets on silver nanoarrays (Ag NRs/M − BN) and showed the detection limit of 1.4 × 10−8 M for bilirubin in blood [18]. Vitamins are organic substances that are required in modest doses for the proper functioning of the metabolism. Among different vitamins (A, B, C, D, E, and K), vitamin B12 is a water-soluble cobalt-containing vitamin, known as cobalamin and plays a crucial function in cellular metabolism and serves as coenzymes in a wide range of anabolic and catabolic enzymatic processes. The most structurally complicated vitamin B12 is cyanocobalamin, which is composed of a corrin ring with a Co atom connected to four nitrogen atoms and two axial ligands. This vitamin plays a key function in the neurological system and is essential in DNA synthesis, the formation of red blood cells and the metabolism of fatty and amino acids [19,20]. The insufficiency of this vitamin leads to various health issues like pernicious anemia, weight loss, nausea, and exhaustion. Jafari et al. prepared an Ag-deposited TiO2 nanotube array (TNTA/Ag) as a SERS substrate for the detection of vitamin B12 up to 0.5 × 10−8 M [21]. Ding et al. prepared carbon dots for vitamin B12 detection via the fluorescence assay method, which showed the detection limit of 0.1 μM [22]. Notably, the electrochemically assisted SERS (EC-SERS) has recently attracted significant attention [23]. The EC-SERS is an ultrasensitive spectro-electrochemistry technique that provides mechanistic and dynamic information on electrochemical interfaces at the molecular level. In EC-SERS measurement, the primary aim is to measure the SERS signal of the analyte molecules adsorbed chemically/physically over the surface of the prepared active SERS substrate by applying an electrochemical field [24]. The EC-SERS significantly changes the SERS signal due to the rearrangement of the double layer, and change in the surface adsorption, coverage, or adsorption orientation of the molecules under the influence of the applied electrochemical field. Qu et al. prepared an Ag-WO3-x film using an Ag nanoparticle for the electrochemical behavior of p-aminothiophenol (PATP) using EC-SERS measurement. The prepared Ag-WO3-x film was used for observing the SERS spectra of a PATP at the metal/semiconductor heterostructures interface under different activated potentials [25]. Bindesri et al. successfully designed a fabric-based SERS-active electrode for the detection of both a standard Raman probe, para-ATP, and a common antibiotic, levofloxacin by using an EC-SERS investigation in a 0.1 M NaF and synthetic urine as supporting electrolyte [26]. The EC-SERS study on TMDs nanostructure is still missing in the literature.

In this article, we have successfully synthesized in-situ grown MoS2 film over conducting carbon paper and FTO coated glass (MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO) via a cost-effective hydrothermal route for the detection of organic dye (R6G) and biomolecules (bilirubin and vitamin B12) at ultralow concentration. The in-situ grown MoS2-FTO shows a higher detection limit due to the good conductivity of FTO substrate that facilitates better charge transfer between the analyte molecule and the substrate. Further, we have developed an EC-SERS biosensor based on prepared SERS substrates for the improved detection of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of MoS2 films over CCP and FTO coated glass substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The in-situ growth of MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO was done using sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) and thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2) (purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. India) as precursors. Firstly, 0.1 M sodium molybdate and 0.2 M thioacetamide were dissolved in 40 ml of deionized (DI) water. Then hydrazine hydrate (3 ml) was poured into it and was probe sonicated for 15 min until the solution turns yellow. Then the solution was placed in a hydrothermal cell for 24 h at 200 °C in a Teflon-lined autoclave of 100 ml capacity with CCP and FTO coated glass dipped into the prepared solution. The autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. At high temperatures, thioacetamide reacts with water forming H2S gas, which reduces sodium molybdate to produce the required MoS2 film. Hydrazine hydrate enhances the reduction process and adds nitrogen functionality. These substrates (MoS2-FTO and MoS2-CCP) were dipped in the mixture of DI water and ethanol (equal proportion) for overnight and then dried at 50 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis of in-situ grown MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO are shown in Fig. 1 .

The microstructure and surface morphology of as-synthesized in-situ grown MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO were observed using field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) – SUPRA 40 from ZEISS at 20 kV. Raman and SERS studies were performed using STR-300 micro-Raman spectrometer of 532 nm laser excitation source, with 1.148 mW laser power and 1.2 cm−1 step size. Another laser excitation source of 633 nm wavelength with 0.368 mW laser power and 0.8 cm−1 step size was also used. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained using the Thermo Fisher spectrometer using sample/KBr pallets.

The solutions of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin of 10−3 M concentrations were prepared using Milli-Q water for the SERS experiment. They were successively diluted to make a series of different concentrations from 10 −4 to 10 −12 M using the serial dilution method. 2 μl of each solution was dropped on the surface of each substrate and then dried at 40 °C for 2 h in a vacuum oven before SERS detection. The aqueous solutions of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin at different concentrations (10 −3 M and 10 −12 M) are shown in Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information. The measurement errors have been reduced by performing multiple SERS measurements at each concentration. The SERS mappings are performed in a selected area of 0.25 μm × 0.25 μm with a step size of 0.05 μm.

The morphological study of the as-prepared MoS2 films was done using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. 2 a, b shows the SEM images of the MoS2-CCP at different magnifications. Fig. 2a clearly shows the growth of MoS2 over different carbon fibers distributed in conducting carbon paper. Fig. 2b shows the high magnification image of MoS2 over each fiber, showing the uniform growth of porous MoS2 nanoparticles. Fig. 2c, d shows the SEM images of MoS2-FTO at different magnifications, which suggest again the distribution of porous MoS2 nanoparticles over the surface of FTO coated glass. Fig. 2e indicates the Raman spectra of 2H–MoS2 films grown over CCP and FTO coated glass using 532 nm excitation wavelength, indicating the characteristic Raman active modes of MoS2 around 380, and 404 cm−1, corresponding to the in-plane Mo–S phonon mode (E1 2g), and out-of-plane S–S mode (A1g), respectively. Furthermore, a weak broad peak appears around 450 cm−1 corresponding to the 2LA(M) mode, a second-order longitudinal acoustic (LA) phonon mode at the M point in the Brillouin zone [27,28]. The presence of these peaks confirms the formation of a hexagonally symmetric crystalline structure of the 2H phase of MoS2 films. The additional peaks at 1343 and 1585 cm−1 for MoS2-CCP, indicate the characteristic D and G bands of carbon paper [29]. The Raman spectra of MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO using 633 nm excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. S2 of Supporting Information. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy study of synthesized MoS2 films is done to identify the available functional groups in prepared MoS2 and the respective FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 2f. The FTIR spectra reveal the existence of bands around 476 and 620 cm−1, corresponding to Mo–S and S–S bond vibrations of MoS2, respectively. Further, the peaks around 1024 and 1128 cm−1 indicate the presence of C–O and SO stretching vibration, respectively. The broad peak around 1403 cm−1 is observed due to O–H bending vibration, while the peak around 1631 cm−1 is observed due to the presence of the carbonyl group (CO). The peaks around 2927 and 3431 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H asymmetric vibration and hydroxyl stretching vibrations, respectively [30].

In order to detect the analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin), hydrothermally in-situ grown MoS2 films have been used as SERS substrates. The details of Raman spectra for bulk R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin are given in section S3 (Fig. S3) of the Supporting Information. The SERS study of R6G and vitamin B12 has been performed with a 532 nm excitation source, while the SERS study of bilirubin has been done using a 633 nm excitation source. Fig. 3 a shows the SERS signals for R6G molecules adsorbed on the surface of MoS2-CCP at different concentrations (10−3 to 10−7 M), clearly indicating that the intensities of different Raman peaks increase with increasing concentrations. The peak positions in SERS spectra of R6G molecules are found to be shifted (blue shift) compared to Raman spectra of bulk R6G and is related to the chemical bond length of the complex formed, orientation of molecules, and its interactions with the SERS substrate. The shorter bond length leads to a higher peak shift, showing stronger bonding and vice versa. This clearly indicates the enhancement in the SERS signal of R6G due to CT between the SERS substrate and R6G molecule [31–33]. Fig. 3b shows the linear plot of SERS intensity (613 cm−1 peak: C–C–C ring) for MoS2-CCP with respect to different concentrations (10−3 to 10−7 M) of R6G molecules. The MoS2 peak is observed to be weaker at higher concentration in SERS measurement due to a larger number of analyte coverage at the surface of MoS2. This feature is observed for all analytes adsorbed over the surface of the MoS2 SERS substrate.

Fig. 3c shows the SERS spectra for R6G at different concentrations (10−6 to 10−10 M) adsorbed on MoS2-FTO, which exhibits a higher detection limit compared to MoS2-CCP. Here the SERS peaks of R6G on MoS2-FTO substrate are found to be shifted more in comparison to that on MoS2-CCP, showing stronger interaction between R6G and MoS2-FTO. This indicates the possibility of more CT and hence a better detection limit for the MoS2-FTO substrate compared to the MoS2-CCP substrate. The SERS spectra of concentrations from 10−3 to 10−5 M are shown in Fig. S4a of the Supporting Information. Fig. 3d shows a good linear response of 614 cm−1 peak with respect to different concentrations of R6G molecules adsorbed on the MoS2-FTO substrate. Further, we have performed SERS mapping with multiple SERS measurements in a given area of the MoS2 substrate, suggesting the nearly uniform distribution of R6G over the surface of MoS2 films grown over CCP and FTO substrates, as shown in Figs. S5 and S6 of the Supporting Information.

Fig. 4 a shows the SERS signals for vitamin B12 molecules adsorbed on the surface of MoS2-CCP at different concentrations (10−3 to 10−5 M). The SERS peaks of vitamin B12 biomolecules are found to be slightly shifted compared to its Raman spectrum. Fig. 4b shows the linear response of SERS peak intensity at 1497 cm−1 (corrin ring vibration mode) with respect to different concentrations (10−3 to 10−5 M) of vitamin B12 molecules adsorbed on MoS2-CCP. Fig. 4c shows the SERS spectra for vitamin B12 adsorbed on MoS2-FTO at different concentrations (10−3 to 10−6 M). Here, the SERS peaks of vitamin B12 on MoS2-FTO substrate are found to be shifted more in comparison to that on MoS2-CCP, again indicating better interaction of the analyte with MoS2-FTO. This again indicates more CT between vitamin B12 molecule and MoS2-FTO SERS substrate and hence better detection limit. Fig. 4d shows the good linear response of peak intensity at 1500 cm−1 with respect to different concentrations of vitamin B12 molecules on MoS2-FTO. In order to confirm the uniformity of the SERS signal, we have performed SERS mapping of vitamin B12 molecules on MoS2 film grown over CCP and FTO substrates, revealing the uniform distribution of vitamin B12 over the surface of MoS2 films, as shown in Figs. S7 and S8 of the Supporting Information.

Fig. 5 a shows the SERS signals for bilirubin molecules adsorbed on the surface of MoS2-CCP at different concentrations (10−3 to 10−6 M). The SERS peak of bilirubin biomolecules is found to be significantly shifted compared to its Raman spectrum, indicating the interaction between the biomolecule and SERS substrate. Fig. 5b shows the linear response of SERS peak intensity at 1612 cm−1 with different concentrations (10−3 to 10−6 M) of bilirubin molecules on MoS2-CCP. Fig. 5c shows the SERS spectra of bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2-FTO substrate at different concentrations (10−6 to 10−10 M). The SERS spectra for 10−3 to 10−5 M concentrations are shown in Fig. S4b of the Supporting Information. As observed for previous analytes, the SERS peaks of bilirubin on MoS2-FTO substrate are found to be more shifted in comparison to that on MoS2-CCP, showing a stronger interaction of bilirubin with MoS2-FTO substrate. This indicates more CT between bilirubin molecule and MoS2-FTO substrate and hence better detection limit on MoS2-FTO SERS substrate. Fig. 5d shows again the good linear response of SERS peak intensity at 1613 cm−1 with concentrations of bilirubin. The SERS mapping image of bilirubin on MoS2 film grown over CCP and FTO substrates, clearly indicates the uniform distribution of bilirubin molecules throughout the region, as shown in Figs. S9 and S10 of the Supporting Information.

The significant SERS signals for all analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin) at low concentrations were obtained for prepared MoS2 films on both substrates (CCP and FTO). This could be due to the porous nature of the large accessible surface area of MoS2 for analytes adsorption and better interaction between analytes and MoS2. The peak positions in SERS spectra of analyte molecules are found to be shifted compared to Raman spectra of bulk analytes, indicating the strong interaction between analytes and SERS substrates [34,35]. The MoS2-FTO SERS substrate shows a better detection limit for all the studied analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin) compared to the MoS2-CCP SERS substrate. As per literature, the reported electrical resistivity of the FTO coated glass is ∼3.5 × 10−4 Ω cm [36], whereas the reported electrical resistivity of conducting carbon paper (TOROY paper) is ∼4.8 × 10−3 Ω cm [37]. As reported in literature, the improved conductivity of SERS substrate facilitates better charge transfer and hence provide better detection [38]. The FTO is having higher electrical conductivity as compared to CCP facilitating a greater number of electrons for charge transfer between analyte molecules and MoS2-FTO substrate as compared to MoS2-CCP substrate.

Further, an FTIR study has also been performed to examine the adsorption of analytes and their interaction with SERS substrates. The FTIR spectra of all analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin) and analytes adsorbed over both SERS substrates are shown in Fig. 6 a–c. The corresponding details of FTIR spectra are given in section S6 of the Supporting Information. The FTIR spectra of R6G and R6G adsorbed on MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO are shown in Fig. 6a. The peak (1631 cm−1) corresponding to the presence of the carbonyl group (CO) in MoS2 is shifted to 1638 and 1644 cm−1 for R6G adsorbed MoS2 films, grown over CCP and FTO coated glass, respectively. The blue shift clearly indicates the strong interaction between R6G molecules and SERS substrates. Similarly, the C–C stretching vibration of vitamin B12 is red-shifted from 1403 cm−1 to 1399 and 1401 cm−1 (Fig. 6b) for vitamin B12 adsorbed on MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO, respectively. Similarly, SO stretching vibrations (1128 cm−1) of MoS2 are red-shifted to 1120 and 1123 cm−1 for bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO, respectively (Fig. 6c). Further, the vibrations of C–N, CH3 groups of bilirubin are red-shifted from 1404 cm−1 to 1399 and 1401 cm−1 for grown over CCP and FTO coated glass, respectively. The peak around 3431 cm−1, representing the hydroxyl group for MoS2 film (Fig. 2f), is blue shifted to 3437, 3435, and 3436 cm−1 for analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin, respectively) adsorbed on MoS2-CCP. The same hydroxyl peak is even largely blue shifted to 3441 cm−1 for all analytes adsorbed on MoS2-FTO. The observed shifts in different vibration modes indicate the better adsorption of analyte molecules and strong interaction with prepared SERS substrates [39–41]. When the analyte molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the SERS substrate, the functional groups of the analyte molecules interact by chemisorption interaction with the functional group present at the surface of the SERS substrate. The peak shift is observed due to the change in the reduced mass because of the additional analyte molecules adsorbed over the surface of the substrate which leads to the change in dipole-dipole coupling between functional groups [42–45]. The functional groups present on the surface of MoS2 and analyte molecules have a significant influence on enhancing the SERS signal. The intrinsic charge transfer mechanism depends on van der Waals interactions, direct chemical bonds, and strong coordination effect between the functional group on the MoS2 film surface and vacant orbital in adsorbed analyte molecules. Some functional groups like CO, CC, and nitrogen-containing groups present either in MoS2 or analyte molecules or both, act as effective charge transfer pathways due to the formation of the direct chemical bond between the MoS2 and analyte molecules [46,47]. The larger shifts in vibrational modes for analytes adsorbed MoS2-FTO suggest its better interaction with analyte molecules and hence the possibility of a better CT process. The better electrical conductivity of FTO coated glass may facilitate the CT process. This may lead to a better detection limit for all analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin) using MoS2-FTO as SERS substrate, as observed in our case. The enhancement in the SERS signals can be obtained due to three possible CT contributions between the substrate and molecules: (i) Ground-state charge transfer (GSCT) due to chemisorption between the SERS substrate and molecules in the ground state without excitation, (ii) Photoinduced charge transfer (PICT) attributed to the CT between the molecule and the substrate with the excitation photons, and (iii) Molecular/exciton transitions due to resonance Raman scattering (RRS) [48–50]. The schematic of the CT mechanism for the enhancement of the SERS signal is shown in Fig. 6d. In the present case, GSCT is observed due to good chemisorption of analyte molecules on SERS substrate and the presence of functional groups at the surface of MoS2 and analyte molecules. This leads to the change in polarizability of the probe molecule and generates new electronic states in the molecule for enhanced CT between the SERS substrate and probe molecule. The PICT process occurs due to strong vibronic coupling (μIC and μVK transitions) between analyte molecules and the SERS substrate. The third contribution is obtained due to the suitable laser excitation source leading to molecular/exciton (μIK and μVC) transitions.

The number of molecules interrogated by laser spot in the normal Raman and SERS measurements has been calculated by the following formula [17,51]:
(1)

R a m a n
=
(
π
r 2

)
ρ
N A
M
(2)
N
S E R S
=
C
V
N A
A
R a m a n
A
S u b

Where
N
R a m a n
is the number of molecules probed in the normal Raman spectrum and
N
S E R S
is the number of molecules probed in the SERS spectrum. The radius of the laser spot size is ‘r’ for 50× objective lens and ‘h’ represents the confocal depth or penetration depth of the laser beam into the sample. The molecular weight and density of the molecules are represented by M and
ρ
, respectively, while
N A
is an Avogadro number and V is the droplet volume. The
A
R a m a n
is a laser spot area when the laser is focused on the sample and
A
S u b
is the area of the dried and spread dropped solution. We have calculated the number of molecules observed at the lowest detectable concentration of each analyte for our SERS measurements. The detailed calculations of number of molecules for each analyte are given in section S7 of Supporting Information. We observe the detection of ∼43350, ∼4033540, and ∼455510 number of molecules per μm2 for R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin, respectively, using MoS2-CCP as SERS substrate. Similarly, we obtain the detection of ∼70 molecules per μm2 for R6G, ∼480370 molecules per μm2 for vitamin B12, and ∼100 molecules per μm2 for bilirubin on MoS2-FTO substrate. Hence, our study demonstrates the ultra-sensitive detection of R6G and bilirubin using hydrothermally in-situ grown MoS2-FTO SERS substrate. We have further calculated the enhancement factor (EF) to measure the SERS enhancement limit for our measurements using the following formula [52,53]-
(3)
E F =
I
S E R S
N
S E R S
I
R a m a n
N
R a m a n

Where
I
R a m a n
and
I
S E R S
are the intensities of normal Raman and SERS signals of molecules. The normal Raman spectra of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin molecules over CCP and FTO coated glass, collected as reference data at 10 −3 M concentration, are shown in section S8 and the detailed calculations of EF are given in section S9 of the Supporting Information. The maximum EFs of 5.92 × 103, 9.25 × 101, and 8.43 × 102 are observed with MoS2-CCP as SERS substrate for R6G, vitamin B12 and bilirubin molecules, respectively. The maximum EFs of 2.42 × 106, 4.18 × 102, and 2.78 × 106 have been obtained for R6G, vitamin B12 and bilirubin molecules, respectively, using MoS2-FTO as SERS substrate. Thus, we observe superior sensing ability (high EF) of MoS2-FTO as SERS substrate, especially for R6G and bilirubin due to excellent charge transfer between analyte molecules and SERS substrate as discussed above. A comparison of the SERS detection limit of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin on different SERS substrates with previously reported works is given in Table 1 .

Next, we demonstrate the electrochemically assisted SERS measurement of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin on both prepared SERS substrates. The photograph of the designed EC-SERS cell and experimental details are given in section S10 of the Supporting Information. We have a limitation of using a 50× LWD objective lens for performing EC-SERS. Hence, we have initially examined the SERS signals of analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin) over both SERS substrates at the lowest detected concentration with a 50× LWD objective lens, as shown in Fig. S13 of the Supporting Information. Further, we have examined the EC-SERS signals of SERS substrates immersed in analyte solutions, with application of electrochemical potential. The SERS signal of substrate immersed in analyte solutions without electrochemical potential is also provided in Fig. S13 of the Supporting Information, showing weaker signals. In order to examine the redox behavior of analytes with prepared SERS substrates, we have performed the cyclic voltammetry (CV) study for MoS2-CCP and MoS2-FTO. Fig. 7 a shows CV curves of MoS2-CCP substrate at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for analytes (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin) in 0.1 M KCl solution. It clearly shows that the oxidation peaks are observed at approximately −0.25 V (R6G), −0.16 V (vitamin B12), and −0.14 V (bilirubin) in a potential range of −0.8 to 0.2 V. Further, we have performed the EC-SERS at different electrochemical potentials of −0.8, −0.6, −0.4, −0.2, 0.0, 0.2 V and at oxidation potential for particular analyte solution with respect to Ag/AgCl electrode. Fig. 7b–d shows the EC-SERS spectra of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin with MoS2-CCP substrate, respectively. The spectra clearly show that the SERS intensities of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2-CCP substrate gradually increase up to oxidation potential and further decrease with increasing electrochemical potential. The SERS signal is enhanced due to the change in orientation, rearrangement, and density of the analyte molecules at the interface of MoS2 and analyte under applied electrochemical potential. At the oxidation potential, the maximum charge transfer occurs between the MoS2 and analyte (R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin), therefore the SERS signal enhancement is maximum at the oxidation potential. Similarly, we have performed the EC-SERS of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin adsorbed on MoS2-FTO substrate, as shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8a shows CV curves of MoS2-FTO substrate for R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin molecules under the same condition. It clearly shows that the oxidation peaks are observed at approximately −0.25 V (R6G), −0.16 V (vitamin B12), and −0.14 V (bilirubin) in a potential range of −0.8 to 0.2V. Fig. 8b–d shows the EC-SERS spectra of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin with MoS2-FTO substrate, respectively. Again, at oxidation potential, the EC-SERS signal of analytes is found maximum with MoS2-FTO substrate. The EC-SERS spectra of analytes in the lowest detected concentration at oxidation potential is compared with SERS spectra, as shown in Fig. S13 of the Supporting Information. This indicates that the EC-SERS signal for each analyte at oxidation potential is stronger than the normal SERS signal of analytes with both substrates.

Fig. 9 a, b shows the variation of EC-SERS signal intensity with applied electrochemical potential, which further confirms that the signal is maximum only at the oxidation potential in the corresponding analyte solution. The schematic band diagram of the photon-driven charge transfer between MoS2 to analyte molecules in the EC-SERS system is shown in Fig. 9c. Normally, the electron moves from the valence band (VB) edge to the conduction band (CB) edge of MoS2 upon light illumination in SERS. However, the photon energy is insufficient to have an electron transfer from VB edge of MoS2 to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of analyte molecules. When a positive electrochemical potential is applied on the substrate, the VB edge of MoS2 shifts downwards, which further reduces the probability of charge transfer from VB to CB itself. Once the negative electrochemical potential is applied, the VB edge of MoS2 shifts upward. At oxidation potential (Vox), the VB edge upward shifting is such that it facilitates charge transfer from the VB edge of MoS2 to the LUMO level of analyte molecules upon light illumination. This provides the maximum EC-SERS signal at oxidation potential [54].

In summary, we demonstrate novel, hydrothermally synthesized in-situ grown MoS2 films over different conducting substrates (conducting carbon paper and FTO coated glass) as SERS and EC-SERS biosensors for the detection of R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin. The highest detection limits of 10−10 M, 10−6 M, and 10−10 M concentrations with EFs of 2.42 × 106, 4.18 × 102, and 2.78 × 106 have been obtained for R6G, vitamin B12, and bilirubin, respectively, using MoS2-FTO as SERS substrate. The better SERS detection efficiency of MoS2-FTO compared to MoS2-CCP may be attributed to the better charge transfer between MoS2 and more conducting FTO coated glass. The proposed platform offers great potential for biomedical applications, particularly in clinical diagnostics and point-of-care testing, owing to its high sensitivity, and compatibility with portable instrumentation. The electrochemical-assisted SERS (EC-SERS) signals are found stronger than SERS signals due to the better chemical adsorption and orientation of the analyte molecules on the surface of the substrate under the influence of applied electrochemical potential. Our study paves the way for cost-effective TMDs based EC-SERS biosensors with ultrahigh sensitivity.

Rohit Kumar Gupta: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ankita Singh: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ashish Kumar Mishra: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support of IIT (BHU). The work was financially supported by SERB, India (Grant no. CRG/2020/002186).

ce:section.ce:para:
